india.name: India
india.primer: The Strait of Hormuz - a narrow sea lane between Iran and Oman that carries about a fifth of the world's seaborne oil - has become the front line of this year's US-Iran war. Over recent days the fighting moved from threats to direct hits on commercial tankers and their crews. India has no soldiers in this fight, but it has people: Indians are among the largest pools of merchant-navy crew on the planet, so the world's oil tankers are heavily staffed by Indian sailors.

stories.title: India's biggest private bank posts a strong quarter: ICICI Bank's profit jumps ~16% to ₹14,805 crore, beating forecasts as loans grow and bad loans shrink
stories.moot: A big bank's earnings are a stethoscope on the economy: because banks make money by lending, rising profits alongside shrinking bad loans mean businesses and households are borrowing and paying back — a healthy sign. And a bank's true engine is its net interest margin, the spread between what it charges borrowers and pays savers, so watch that spread and the bad-loan ratio, not just the headline profit.

stories.title: India clears Wegovy to treat fatty-liver disease — the first weight-loss jab approved for a silent condition that afflicts nearly 4 in 10 Indian adults
stories.moot: A medicine's value often expands far beyond the problem it was built for. Because obesity, type-2 diabetes and fatty-liver disease share the same metabolic roots, a single drug designed to curb appetite can end up treating a whole cluster of linked conditions — which is why one class of injections is quietly reshaping the treatment of several of the world's biggest chronic diseases at once.

stories.title: Gujarat is India's most investment-friendly state: NITI Aayog's first-ever ranking scores all 36 states and UTs — Maharashtra and Tamil Nadu follow
stories.moot: Governments increasingly use public rankings as a low-cost tool of reform: turning performance into a visible league table creates competition and political pressure to improve without spending a rupee. But such indices also tend to reward places that are already ahead, which can quietly deepen the gap between richer and poorer regions.

stories.title: Axis Bank's profit jumps 23% to ₹7,114 crore and bad loans shrink — a reassuring quarter from one of India's biggest private banks
stories.moot: A bank's profit is only half the story — the other half is the quality of its loans. Rising earnings paired with shrinking bad loans is the healthy combination, because a bank can always boost profits temporarily by lending recklessly, only to pay for it later when those loans go bad. Watching non-performing assets alongside profit tells you whether the growth is real and durable.

stories.title: US senators push a bill to slap 100% tariffs on India for buying Russian oil — New Delhi says it's 'closely following'
stories.moot: Modern economic pressure increasingly works through third parties: rather than only sanctioning an adversary directly, powers threaten that country's trading partners, turning them into pressure points to isolate the target. It's also a reminder that cheap energy can carry a hidden geopolitical price — a bargain barrel today can become a diplomatic liability tomorrow.

stories.title: Reliance's profit 'falls 25%' — but only because of a one-off a year ago: revenue still jumped 25% as oil, Jio and retail powered India's biggest company
stories.moot: A falling profit number isn't always bad news — you have to compare like with like. One-time gains can inflate a past quarter and make normal, healthy growth look like a decline. When a headline profit and the revenue trend point in opposite directions, the revenue and operating-profit (EBITDA) lines usually tell the truer story of whether a business is actually growing.

stories.title: India's biggest mutual-fund house goes public: SBI Funds Management's ₹9,813-crore IPO closes ~42 times oversubscribed, with big institutions bidding ~140x
stories.moot: How a share sale is structured tells you where the money flows. In an 'offer for sale', the cash goes to the owners cashing out, not to the company — so a wildly oversubscribed IPO can be a great deal for the sellers while the business itself gets nothing new to grow with. And when institutions bid far more eagerly than retail investors, it's a clue about which theme — here, Indians pouring savings into mutual funds — the smart money is betting on.

stories.title: Wipro's profit goes flat and its outlook stays gloomy — India's IT giant signals the sector's slump isn't over as AI reshapes the business
stories.moot: A single company's results are a window onto a whole industry — and onto a bigger shift. Wipro's flat profit and cautious tone show how a business built on selling human hours gets squeezed twice over: once when clients cut budgets, and again when a new technology like AI threatens to do some of that work itself. The firms that thrive will be the ones that sell the AI rather than get replaced by it.

stories.title: India draws up a $51bn 'make it at home' hit-list — Modi orders a push to replace imports and shield the economy from oil shocks, a weak rupee and China's grip on supplies
stories.moot: A big import bill isn't just an economic number — it's a vulnerability. Every essential thing a country must buy from abroad is a lever someone else can pull, through prices, a weak currency, or an export ban. That's why nations under pressure try to make more of their own critical goods: self-reliance is as much about security as about savings.

stories.title: Files from India's biggest nuclear plant, Kudankulam, turn up on the dark web — a contractor admits a 'partial breach' as CERT-In investigates
stories.moot: A system is only as secure as its weakest link — and for guarded facilities like nuclear plants, that link is increasingly the outside contractors and cloud servers that hold their data, not the fortified site itself. Cybersecurity has to cover the whole supply chain of vendors, or the front door stays locked while the back door swings open.

stories.title: Deadly crush at Puri's Rath Yatra again: at least one devotee dead and 100+ hospitalised in a crowd surge — a year after a stampede here killed three
stories.moot: At mass gatherings the crowd itself is the risk: past a certain density, a single stumble or a broken barricade can cascade into a fatal crush, and packing more police in doesn't help if the space and the flow of people aren't designed for the numbers. Preventing disasters is about managing density and exits in advance, not reacting once the surge has begun.

stories.title: India's fiercest federal fight is back: the Modi government revives the 'delimitation' bill that could shift power from the south to the north — and Kharge demands an all-party meeting
stories.moot: How you count people decides who holds power. Tying representation to raw population sounds fair, but it can quietly punish regions that did what was asked of them — so in a diverse federation, 'one person, one vote' constantly collides with 'don't let the biggest states swallow the smaller ones'. That tension is the permanent balancing act of any large democracy.

stories.title: India's first private rocket goes for orbit: Skyroot sets July 18 for the maiden launch of Vikram-1 from Sriharikota
stories.moot: Space is shifting from a government-only endeavour into a private industry, and the first orbital launch is the gate a private space sector must clear before it can compete. When a country lets startups build rockets, launches tend to get cheaper and more frequent — the same shift SpaceX drove in the US, now playing out in India.

stories.title: India's inflation jumps past the RBI's target for the first time in 18 months — pricier fuel and food push June prices up 4.38%
stories.moot: A central bank's interest-rate decisions swing on one number — inflation versus its target — so when prices cross that line, borrowing costs and your loan EMIs are the next thing in play. And inflation is often imported: a war thousands of miles away can raise the price of the fuel in your tank and the food on your plate.

stories.title: India's biggest trade deal with the West goes live: the India-UK free-trade pact kicks in — cheaper Scotch and British cars coming in, near-zero tariffs for Indian exports going out
stories.moot: Free-trade deals work by trading away protection: each side gives up some of its tariffs to win access to the other's market. The winners are exporters and the consumers of the newly cheaper goods; the real test is always whether the jobs and sales gained outweigh the home industries newly exposed to competition.

stories.title: India's first hydrogen train is here: Modi flags off a zero-emission trainset from Haryana on Friday
stories.moot: The hardest emissions to cut are in transport and heavy industry, where batteries and wires don't easily reach — which is why countries are testing hydrogen there first. A pilot like this is less about one train than about proving a whole new supply chain (fuel, storage, refuelling) can work at all.

stories.title: Sonam Wangchuk's hunger strike hits Day 18 — and the Delhi High Court orders the government to keep him alive
stories.moot: In a democracy the right to protest and the state's duty to preserve life can collide — and when they do, it is often a court, not the government or the protester, that ends up setting the terms.

stories.title: Brain drain at India's space agency: 100+ ISRO scientists — many on the Gaganyaan crewed mission — have quit, so the government is now freezing exits
stories.moot: When a country opens a state monopoly to private competition, its own government experts suddenly become poachable — talent flows to wherever the pay and upside are best. Freezing people in place treats the symptom, not the cause; retention is ultimately about incentives, not restrictions.

stories.title: India's most famous central banker lands a US seat: ex-RBI chief Raghuram Rajan is co-leading a task force to overhaul the Federal Reserve
stories.moot: A central bank's balance sheet is a hidden throttle on the world's money supply, and because US interest rates anchor global capital, who sits on the Fed's review panels can matter to a saver in Mumbai almost as much as to one in Chicago.

stories.title: The Iran war reaches India's tax code: New Delhi almost doubles the 'windfall tax' on diesel and jet-fuel exports to ₹15.5 and ₹14.5 a litre as oil spikes
stories.moot: A windfall tax is how a government claws back profits a company earns from luck — here, war-driven oil prices — rather than from skill; and because it's tied to a formula, watching it move is like reading a thermometer for how hot the underlying commodity has become.

stories.title: NEET-UG leak, decoded in court: CBI says 111 of 136 questions found on a Latur tutor's phone matched the exam's secret master set
stories.moot: A high-stakes exam is only as trustworthy as the weakest human link in the chain that writes, translates, prints and guards it — one insider can void a test for millions, which is why security, not just difficulty, defines a fair exam.

stories.title: A Bengaluru-built AI startup becomes India's newest unicorn: Emergent raises $130m at a $1.5bn valuation — about a year after launch
stories.moot: AI is collapsing the cost and time of building software: a company barely a year old can reach $120m in annualised revenue by letting non-coders 'describe' apps into existence — but the same wave that mints new startups erodes the old business of selling human coding hours.

stories.title: Gautam Adani swears there was no 'deal': India's tycoon tells a US court nothing was promised to get his bribery case dropped
stories.moot: When a government drops a case against a powerful person, a court can still probe whether a hidden trade-off drove the decision — a reminder that a case being dismissed is not the same as being proven innocent.

stories.title: Tata wants to build ships in Kerala: the group behind Tata Steel and Jaguar seeks approval for a ₹10,000 crore ($1bn) shipyard — its first-ever move into shipbuilding
stories.moot: A country can dominate the *use* of an industry while barely participating in *making* it — India moves enormous volumes of goods by sea yet builds almost none of the ships. Closing that kind of gap needs patient, capital-heavy players, which is why a conglomerate's balance sheet, not a startup's, tends to be what finally enters industries like shipbuilding.

stories.title: India's market watchdog polices itself: SEBI bars its own board members from buying stocks and sets up a formal recusal rulebook
stories.moot: A referee is only as trusted as their neutrality — institutions that police others eventually have to prove they can police themselves.

stories.title: India moves to bring streaming films under the Censor Board — planning to force OTT movies to get clearance before release, after pulling 'Satluj/Punjab 95'
stories.moot: New media usually enjoys a regulation-light honeymoon, but once it grows big and influential, governments tend to extend older rules to cover it — a young platform's freedom is often temporary.

stories.title: India bets big on chips: Modi's cabinet clears ₹1.27 lakh crore for 'Semicon 2.0' to build a homegrown semiconductor industry
stories.moot: Building an industry from scratch takes more than one flagship factory — you need the whole chain, from raw materials to packaging to skilled workers. Subsidising every link, not just the headline plant, is how countries try to turn ambition into genuine self-reliance.

stories.title: Cracks in Bengal's ruling party: veteran leader Madan Mitra quits TMC posts for a rebel camp as Mamata Banerjee digs in behind her nephew
stories.moot: Ruling parties are often threatened less by the opposition than from within — when investigative pressure and a succession grievance combine before an election, internal cracks can decide outcomes more than any rival's campaign.

stories.title: 15 Indian tourists killed as a boat capsizes off Vietnam's Phu Quoc — Modi condoles, Hanoi orders a probe
stories.moot: Tourism safety is only as strong as its least-regulated operator: when a destination gets popular fast, visitor numbers can outrun the rules for boats, buses and guides — so one lapse can produce an outsized, cross-border tragedy.

stories.title: India's car market just had its best-ever start to a year: a record 12.74 lakh vehicles shipped in Q1, up ~26%
stories.moot: Car sales are a window into the wider economy: because most vehicles are bought on credit, a strong quarter usually means households feel confident and loans are cheap. But watch the gap between factory 'dispatches' and real buyers — shipping cars to dealers is not the same as selling them.

stories.title: India bets bigger on 'Make in India' phones: Cabinet clears a ₹62,500 crore Mobile Phone Manufacturing Scheme to deepen local production and exports
stories.moot: Assembling a product and truly manufacturing it are not the same thing — a country only captures real value, jobs and export earnings when it makes the components, not just clicks them together, which is why 'value addition' is the number worth watching.

stories.title: Files on India's biggest nuclear plant, Kudankulam, surface on the dark web after a contractor is hacked — including alleged blueprints
stories.moot: Your security is only as strong as your weakest supplier — attackers increasingly breach the small contractor or data-centre vendor rather than the well-guarded main target, because sensitive data flows to those third parties too. Guarding the 'front door' of a critical facility means little if its blueprints sit on a poorly secured vendor's server.

stories.title: India clears its biggest chip bet yet: Cabinet approves 'Semiconductor Mission 2.0' with a Rs 1.27 lakh crore war chest to make more of its own chips
stories.moot: Real industrial self-reliance means climbing the value chain, not just assembling imported parts. Funding the hard, high-margin steps — chip design and materials — matters far more than just hosting the final assembly line.

stories.title: India bets on urea self-reliance: the Cabinet approves a new investment policy to build seven fertiliser plants and cut import dependence
stories.moot: Self-reliance in an essential is really about controlling your exposure to shocks. When you import both a product (urea) and the raw material used to make it (gas), your food-growing chain is hostage to distant wars and prices you can't control — so building domestic capacity is less about pride than about insulating farmers and the budget from forces abroad.

stories.title: India's Air India crash probe enters its final stage: investigators finish the cockpit-voice transcript and a 'psychological autopsy,' with the draft report due in October
stories.moot: Air-crash investigations are deliberately slow and layered — machines, procedures and people are all examined before any cause is named. Early leaks and speculation almost always outrun the evidence, which is exactly why final reports take a year or more and why an unproven theory should never be treated as the conclusion.

stories.title: India's E20 petrol backlash boils over: drivers say the mandatory 20% ethanol blend cuts mileage and harms older engines
stories.moot: A policy can be right for the country and still hurt the individual — and that gap is where politics happens. Cutting oil imports with ethanol helps the national balance sheet, but forcing one fuel on a fleet built for another shifts the cost onto ordinary owners as worse mileage and wear. When a reform removes choice overnight, the backlash is often about the 'how', not the goal.

stories.title: India bans imports of goods made with forced labour — a first, and a move aimed at heading off US tariffs
stories.moot: Trade rules double as diplomatic signals. A country will often adopt a domestic standard partly to pre-empt another country's penalty — so a new regulation at home can be a defensive move aimed abroad. Watch the timing: when a rule appears just before a foreign deadline, the audience may be foreign as much as domestic.

stories.title: Sensex falls 561 points as surging oil and the West Asia flare-up drag banks and autos
stories.moot: For an economy that imports most of its oil, the crude price is a hidden hand on the stock market. A Middle East shock doesn't just raise fuel costs — it weakens the currency, stokes inflation, dims rate-cut hopes and triggers foreign selling, all of which land hardest on banks and carmakers. One barrel of oil moves many prices at once.

stories.title: Rupee breaches 96 to the dollar as crude climbs and the RBI's forex boost fades
stories.moot: For a country that imports its energy in dollars, a weak currency and expensive oil compound each other into a double squeeze. The same shock hits twice — once through the barrel's price and again through the exchange rate — which is why an oil-importer's inflation and markets are so exposed to events far from its borders.

stories.title: India's biggest trade deal in years goes live: the India–UK free-trade pact (CETA) takes effect today, targeting ~$100bn in two-way trade by 2030
stories.moot: A trade deal's value isn't in the signing — it's in the switch-on. Tariffs are a hidden tax that quietly prices exporters out of foreign markets; removing them can hand an industry a lasting cost edge. And spreading trade across many partners is itself a form of insurance and bargaining power — the more doors a country can sell through, the less any single partner can dictate terms.

democratic-republic-of-congo.name: Democratic Republic of Congo
democratic-republic-of-congo.primer: Ebola is a severe, often-fatal virus spread through the bodily fluids of infected people; it causes internal bleeding and organ failure and was first identified near the DRC's Ebola River in 1976. There are several species of the virus. Two approved treatments exist — but only for the Zaire species. The current outbreak is the Bundibugyo species, which has sickened people since 2007 and against which no drug has ever been formally tested. Running such a trial is hard because outbreaks are remote, fast-moving and dangerous.

stories.title: First-ever treatment trial for this Ebola strain begins at record speed
stories.moot: Having a drug is not the same as knowing it works; without controlled trials, medicine is guesswork — so the ability to run a rigorous study fast, in the middle of a crisis, is itself a life-saving capability.

stories.title: DR Congo Ebola outbreak remains one of the deadliest in years
stories.moot: Large outbreaks strain fragile health systems and carry cross-border risk.

stories.title: Ebola outbreak in DR Congo passes 300 deaths
stories.moot: Beyond the human tragedy, large Ebola outbreaks strain fragile health systems and carry the risk — however contained so far — of spreading across borders.

africa.name: Africa
africa.primer: Ebola is a severe, often-fatal disease that spreads through contact with the blood and body fluids of an infected person, causing fever, bleeding and organ failure. The Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC), a vast, poor country in central Africa with a weak health system and ongoing armed conflict in its east, has suffered more Ebola outbreaks than anywhere on Earth — this is its 17th. Crucially, there are several different types ('strains') of Ebola virus, and the two vaccines the world has stockpiled were built to fight the most common one, the Zaire strain. This outbreak is caused by a different, rarer strain — Bundibugyo — for which no vaccine or drug has ever been licensed.

stories.title: The fastest-spreading Ebola outbreak ever is raging in DR Congo — 2,000+ cases and 754 dead, and there's no vaccine that works on this strain
stories.moot: A medical breakthrough only protects you against the exact problem it was built for. The world has powerful Ebola vaccines — but they target one strain, so a different strain can leave responders back at square one. Preparedness isn't a single tool; it's having tools for each version of a threat, and a fast, trusted public-health system to use them.

stories.title: First-ever treatment trial for this Ebola strain begins at record speed
stories.moot: Having a drug is not the same as knowing it works; without controlled trials, medicine is guesswork — so the ability to run a rigorous study fast, in the middle of a crisis, is itself a life-saving capability.

stories.title: DR Congo Ebola outbreak remains one of the deadliest in years
stories.moot: Large outbreaks strain fragile health systems and carry cross-border risk.

stories.title: Ebola outbreak in DR Congo passes 300 deaths
stories.moot: Beyond the human tragedy, large Ebola outbreaks strain fragile health systems and carry the risk — however contained so far — of spreading across borders.

south-korea.name: South Korea
south-korea.primer: SK Hynix is a South Korean maker of memory chips — the components that store data and feed the graphics processors (like Nvidia's) that train AI. As AI demand exploded, so did demand for the high-bandwidth memory Hynix specialises in. It listed shares in the US to tap the world's deepest investor pool and fund new factories. Meanwhile, after two years of an AI-led bull market, some investors are starting to ask whether the theme is overheating.

stories.title: AI's reckoning gets real — 200+ experts demand action on its economic impact, the White House scrambles on power costs, and SK Hynix crashes 15%
stories.moot: A booming industry and an overheating trade are different things — real money can keep pouring into a technology's build-out even as the stocks that led it wobble and its hidden costs (power, jobs) come due. Watch where capital rotates next, not just the headline demand.

stories.title: SK Hynix's record chip debut soars 13% — even as Wall Street starts whispering 'AI fatigue'
stories.moot: A booming industry and an overheating trade are different things — record money can pour into a sector's real build-out even as the stocks that led it start to tire, so watch where capital rotates next, not just the headline demand.

stories.title: SK Hynix raises $26.5bn in the biggest-ever foreign debut on the Nasdaq
stories.moot: Watch where the big money actually goes: a record capital raise into one industry is a concrete, high-conviction signal about where investors think demand is heading — louder than any forecast.

stories.title: NVIDIA and SK hynix deepen the AI-memory alliance
stories.moot: Modern AI runs on a tight stack of GPUs plus ultra-fast memory.

japan.name: Japan
japan.primer: For years Japan treated crypto mainly as a 'means of payment' under its Payment Services Act — a law written to police money transfers, not investments. That left Japanese crypto traders facing some of the world's harshest taxes (gains treated as 'miscellaneous income' at rates up to 55%) and gave regulators no clean legal route to approve mainstream investment products. Japan was one of the earliest crypto hubs and the scene of huge exchange hacks (Mt. Gox, Coincheck), so it has long wrestled with how to supervise the asset.

stories.title: Japan just moved crypto into the same rulebook as stocks and bonds — clearing the path for Bitcoin ETFs and a much lower tax
stories.moot: How an asset is legally classified shapes everything around it — the tax you pay, who is allowed to sell it to you, and what products can be built on top. Moving crypto from a 'payments' law to an 'investments' law is what turns a fringe token into something a pension fund or a cautious saver can actually hold.

stories.title: India signs a trade deal with New Zealand and hardens its terror line with Japan
stories.moot: When the biggest trading power turns protectionist, mid-sized economies hedge by signing more deals with each other — trade diversification is a country's insurance policy against a single partner's mood swings.

stories.title: Japan's PM visits India to deepen an Indo-Pacific partnership
stories.moot: For India, closer ties with Japan mean investment, technology and a stronger hand in Asia's balance of power.

europe.name: Europe
europe.primer: NATO is the 32-nation military alliance that binds the US, Canada and most of Europe under a promise of mutual defence. At last year's summit Trump extracted a pledge that members would raise defence spending toward 5% of GDP (the size of their economies) — a huge jump. This year's summit, in Turkey's capital Ankara, is where he tries to make them honour it. It convenes days after Russia's heaviest barrage of the war exposed that Ukraine's US-made Patriot air-defence systems — the only weapon that can reliably stop Russia's fastest 'ballistic' missiles — have almost run out of interceptor missiles.

stories.title: Britain gets a new prime minister without an election: Andy Burnham is declared Labour leader and will replace Keir Starmer in Downing Street on Monday
stories.moot: In a parliamentary democracy the prime minister is simply the leader of the ruling party, not a directly elected president — so a country can change its head of government in the middle of a term, without any national election, the moment the governing party picks a new chief. And rapid leadership churn (seven prime ministers in ten years) is usually a symptom, not a cause: it reflects deeper economic strain and public frustration that no single leader has been able to fix.

stories.title: Britain nationalises British Steel — the government seizes its last major steel mill from its Chinese owner to keep the country able to make its own steel
stories.moot: Some industries are treated as strategic, not just commercial: when a country decides it must be able to make its own steel, energy or chips, it will pay to keep that capability even if the business itself loses money. The market alone doesn't protect things a nation considers essential to its security — which is why governments sometimes step in and buy them.

stories.title: The EU orders Google to crack open Android and Search — rival AI assistants get 'Hey Google'-style access and competitors get its search data
stories.moot: In digital markets, the real moat isn't a single product — it's control of the gateway (the operating system, the search box) plus the mountain of data that flows through it. That is exactly why regulators are shifting from breaking up companies to forcing them to share access and data: prying open the gateway can do more for competition than any fine.

stories.title: Zelensky fires Ukraine's popular 'drone-war' defence minister — and mass protests erupt in Kyiv as parliament installs a new prime minister
stories.moot: Even under wartime rule, a leader's power ultimately rests on public trust — and removing a popular, successful official without explaining why can spark the very instability the move was meant to avoid. Transparency isn't a courtesy in a crisis; it's what keeps authority intact when people can't simply vote you out.

stories.title: Uber makes a $14.8bn move to swallow Delivery Hero — a bid to build the world's biggest food-delivery firm outside China
stories.moot: In platform businesses, markets tend to collapse toward one or two dominant players — so after a long, loss-making land grab, the endgame is consolidation: the profitable survivor buys the rest to own the network. And big acquirers now design deals to pass antitrust review, pre-selling the overlapping pieces before regulators even ask.

stories.title: France legalises assisted dying: after 14 years of debate, the National Assembly passes a law letting terminally ill adults take lethal medication
stories.moot: Big social change often turns on procedure as much as persuasion: when a two-chamber legislature deadlocks, the rules about which house gets the 'final say' can decide an outcome the moral argument alone never settled.

stories.title: India's biggest trade deal with the West goes live: the India-UK free-trade pact kicks in — cheaper Scotch and British cars coming in, near-zero tariffs for Indian exports going out
stories.moot: Free-trade deals work by trading away protection: each side gives up some of its tariffs to win access to the other's market. The winners are exporters and the consumers of the newly cheaper goods; the real test is always whether the jobs and sales gained outweigh the home industries newly exposed to competition.

stories.title: Britain is about to swap prime ministers again: Keir Starmer resigns and Andy Burnham is set to take over on Monday — the UK's 7th leader in just over a decade
stories.moot: In a parliamentary democracy a prime minister doesn't need to lose a national election to lose power — they govern only as long as their own party's MPs back them. When the party turns, the leader falls, and the country's top job can change hands without voters casting a single ballot.

stories.title: Ann Widdecombe, a well-known British politician, is killed in a 'targeted attack' — and counter-terror police take over the case
stories.moot: Political violence is measured not just by the one life lost but by the fear it spreads — when a public figure is attacked, the real target is open debate itself, which is why states treat such acts as terrorism rather than ordinary crime.

stories.title: The company that makes the machines that make every advanced AI chip just raised its outlook — again: ASML lifts its 2026 forecast on 'extremely strong' orders
stories.moot: To judge whether a boom is real, look at the base of its supply chain, not the loudest stock. The company selling the one essential tool everyone needs — the 'picks and shovels' of the gold rush — gives a cleaner read on true demand than any single product-maker, because everyone in the industry has to go through it.

stories.title: Volkswagen warns of up to 50,000 more job cuts to survive
stories.moot: In a fast technology shift, incumbents are often crippled not by running out of money but by a cost structure built for the old world — high wages and legacy factories become an anchor when nimbler rivals redefine the product.

stories.title: Hungary's parliament votes to oust Orban's ally from the presidency
stories.moot: Winning an election is only the start of taking power; entrenched leaders leave loyalists embedded across courts, the presidency and agencies, so the real struggle is often the slow, legally messy fight to control the institutions afterward.

stories.title: Summer of extremes: wildfire hits Paris's Fontainebleau forest, 10 still missing in Spain, and warm rivers cut French nuclear output
stories.moot: A warming climate turns ordinary 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can burn suburbs that never burned before, strain grids, cut power output and threaten harvests at once, because infrastructure was built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Heat and fire on two continents — wildfire reaches Paris's outskirts, a 'dangerous' US heat dome builds, and 2,700 heat deaths counted in England and Wales
stories.moot: A warming climate turns ordinary 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can kill thousands indoors, burn suburbs, strain grids and threaten harvests at once, because societies and infrastructure were built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Deadly heat spans two continents — Spain's wildfire kills 12+ as a US heat dome bakes two-thirds of the country
stories.moot: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can kill in a wildfire, close landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids across whole continents at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Europe's heat emergency turns deadly — Spanish wildfire kills 12+, Paris shuts the Eiffel Tower early
stories.moot: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same heatwave can kill in a wildfire, close national landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Deadly heat and storms: a Spanish wildfire kills 12 as Typhoon Bavi bears down on Japan
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk: extreme weather is no longer just a humanitarian story but a driver of insurance costs, farm output and even chip supply — which is why markets increasingly watch the forecast.

stories.title: NATO summit wraps: €70bn for Ukraine, and Trump lets Kyiv build its own Patriot missiles
stories.moot: Owning the factory beats owning the stockpile — a licence to build your own weapons changes a country's leverage far more than any single shipment, but manufacturing capacity takes years to stand up, so the relief lags the announcement.

stories.title: Le Pen launches her 2027 campaign — ankle monitor and all
stories.moot: In modern democracies the exact terms of a sentence — which months are suspended, whether a ban is backdated — can decide whether a frontrunner is knocked out or set free, so 'political risk' now includes judicial calendars as much as ballot boxes.

stories.title: NATO's Ankara summit pledges €70bn for Ukraine — but much of it is old money, and the US won't pay
stories.moot: Read the fine print on big numbers: a headline aid figure can be mostly old money relabelled, and who actually writes the cheque (here, Europe, not the US) tells you more about real commitment than the total does.

stories.title: French court hands Le Pen a lifeline — conviction upheld, but the ban shortened enough to run in 2027
stories.moot: In modern democracies a courtroom can matter as much as a ballot box — but the exact length of a sentence, down to which months are suspended or backdated, can decide whether a frontrunner is knocked out or set free. Details are destiny.

stories.title: NATO summit opens in Ankara — Trump demands the money, Ukraine begs for interceptors after its worst night
stories.moot: Alliances are tested not by their promises but by their invoices — a summit reveals how much collective defence a group will actually pay for, and on the battlefield the side that can still shoot down incoming missiles keeps its bargaining power.

stories.title: France's top court rules today on whether Marine Le Pen can run for president
stories.moot: In modern democracies a courtroom can matter as much as a ballot box — a single ruling can remove a frontrunner and reroute a country's politics, which is why 'political risk' includes judicial calendars.

stories.title: Europe's record heat turns deadly and unequal — wildfires force evacuations in France
stories.moot: Climate change shows up first as an infrastructure-and-inequality bill — societies built for yesterday's weather must retrofit grids and homes, and who can afford to adapt, and who can't, becomes a political fight.

stories.title: Russia answers peace talk with its heaviest strike on Kyiv — and Ukraine is running out of interceptors
stories.moot: Diplomacy tends to ratify the facts on the ground, not replace them — which is why a 'peace call' can be followed by a bigger attack. On the battlefield, the side that can still defend its cities keeps its bargaining power; run out of interceptors and you run out of leverage.

stories.title: France decides tomorrow whether Marine Le Pen can run for president
stories.moot: In modern democracies, a courtroom can matter as much as a ballot box — a single ruling can remove a frontrunner and reroute a country's politics, which is why 'political risk' includes judicial calendars.

stories.title: India's Persistent to buy Germany's Nagarro for ₹11,820 crore in an AI-engineering land-grab
stories.moot: When a technology threatens to disrupt an industry, incumbents often respond by buying the capability rather than building it — acquisitions are a way to purchase time and talent you can't grow fast enough internally.

stories.title: Europe's record heat turns deadly and unequal — and forces wildfire evacuations in France
stories.moot: Climate change shows up first as an infrastructure-and-inequality bill: societies built for yesterday's weather must retrofit grids and homes, and who can afford to adapt — and who can't — becomes a political fight.

stories.title: Google loses its last appeal — Europe's €4.1bn Android fine is now final
stories.moot: For a giant, the fine is rarely the point — the lasting cost of an antitrust loss is the precedent that constrains how it's allowed to run its business.

stories.title: Record heat scorches the US and Europe — and strains the power grid
stories.moot: Climate change shows up first as a demand shock — extreme heat spikes electricity use and prices precisely when the grid can least handle it, turning weather into an economic and political problem.

stories.title: Ukraine strikes a major Russian oil terminal in St Petersburg
stories.moot: Modern wars are fought over cash flow — striking the enemy's oil terminals aims at the money that funds the fighting, not just at territory.

stories.title: Europe's deadly heat: France records ~2,025 excess deaths as June records shatter
stories.moot: Climate change shows up first as heat deaths and higher electricity bills — the costs are already here and being paid, not hypothetical or far in the future.

stories.title: AI's reality check: Meta cuts 8,000 jobs, Google loses its €4.1bn appeal, OpenAI's UK plan stalls
stories.moot: Every technology boom hits a gap between promise and delivery — when timelines slip and regulators bite, the most richly-priced leaders are the first to reprice.

stories.title: Russia hits Kyiv with its biggest attack of the war
stories.moot: In modern warfare, the side that runs out of cheap defences first is often the one that loses the war of attrition — a $50,000 drone can force the use of a $2 million interceptor, so cost, not just courage, decides how long a country can hold on.

stories.title: Google loses its record EU antitrust appeal — a €4.1bn bill
stories.moot: Market dominance itself isn't illegal — using it to smother rivals is. Platforms that grow by making their own services the unavoidable default eventually collide with competition law.

stories.title: Killer heat grips the US and Europe as summers turn extreme
stories.moot: Heat is a slow-motion disaster: it rarely makes dramatic footage, but it kills more people and costs economies more than flashier weather events — which is why it's now treated as critical infrastructure risk.

israel.name: Israel
israel.primer: Ali Khamenei ruled Iran as Supreme Leader — its highest political and religious authority — for 36 years. He was assassinated on February 28, 2026, at the very start of a short but intense US-Israel war on Iran. His son, Mojtaba Khamenei, was named successor by Iran's Assembly of Experts in March but has stayed out of public view since. A fragile calm has since returned to the region, including the vital Strait of Hormuz shipping lane.

stories.title: Overshadowed by the Iran war, Israel now controls nearly 70% of Gaza as hospitals go dark
stories.moot: A ceasefire is a label, not a guarantee — what matters is the reality underneath it: who controls the ground and whether basic systems like power and hospitals still function; watch conditions, not just declarations.

stories.title: Nine months into a ceasefire, Israel now controls nearly 70% of Gaza as hospitals go dark
stories.moot: A ceasefire is a label, not a guarantee — what matters is the reality underneath it: who controls the ground and whether basic systems like power and hospitals still function; watch conditions, not just declarations.

stories.title: Iran buries its assassinated Supreme Leader in a week-long show of defiance
stories.moot: Markets care less about who leads a country than about whether the transition is orderly — predictability keeps oil and risk premiums low, while uncertainty at the top of an oil power is priced into the whole world's fuel bill.

new-zealand.name: New Zealand
new-zealand.primer: India is negotiating trade deals with several partners at once — the US, UK, EU and, now, New Zealand — as it tries to widen markets for its goods and services. A free-trade agreement (FTA) is a pact that lowers tariffs (import taxes) so two countries can trade more cheaply. With the US pushing a proposed 12.5% tariff on some Indian goods, New Delhi has taken a firmer negotiating stance, betting its fast-growing market gives it leverage to wait for better terms rather than sign quickly.

stories.title: Trade on two fronts: an 'emboldened' India holds out on a US deal as it inks a 'historic' free-trade pact with New Zealand
stories.moot: Trade negotiation is a game of alternatives — the more partners a country can deal with, the less any single one can dictate terms. India signing an easy FTA with New Zealand while holding firm with the US shows how diversification itself becomes bargaining power.

stories.title: Modi upgrades India–New Zealand ties to a 'strategic partnership' — first Indian PM visit in 40 years
stories.moot: Diplomacy compounds like interest — a run of back-to-back deals (missiles, uranium, trade, partnerships) turns individual handshakes into a strategic web, and the best time to court neglected partners is when a rival's rise makes every friend more valuable.

stories.title: India signs a trade deal with New Zealand and hardens its terror line with Japan
stories.moot: When the biggest trading power turns protectionist, mid-sized economies hedge by signing more deals with each other — trade diversification is a country's insurance policy against a single partner's mood swings.

france.name: France
france.primer: Whether the state should let a doctor help a dying person end their life is one of the oldest, most divisive questions in medical ethics — pitting personal autonomy and mercy against fears that the vulnerable, elderly or disabled could be pushed toward death. France has argued over it for more than a decade across successive governments, with reformers pointing to neighbours who already allow it and opponents (including much of the Catholic church and some doctors' groups) warning of a slippery slope. The bill had already cleared the National Assembly in earlier readings but kept hitting resistance in the Senate, France's upper house.

stories.title: France legalises assisted dying: after 14 years of debate, the National Assembly passes a law letting terminally ill adults take lethal medication
stories.moot: Big social change often turns on procedure as much as persuasion: when a two-chamber legislature deadlocks, the rules about which house gets the 'final say' can decide an outcome the moral argument alone never settled.

stories.moot: A warming climate turns ordinary 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can burn suburbs that never burned before, strain grids, cut power output and threaten harvests at once, because infrastructure was built for a cooler normal.

stories.moot: A warming climate turns ordinary 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can kill thousands indoors, burn suburbs, strain grids and threaten harvests at once, because societies and infrastructure were built for a cooler normal.

stories.moot: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same heatwave can kill in a wildfire, close national landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.

stories.moot: In modern democracies the exact terms of a sentence — which months are suspended, whether a ban is backdated — can decide whether a frontrunner is knocked out or set free, so 'political risk' now includes judicial calendars as much as ballot boxes.

stories.moot: In modern democracies a courtroom can matter as much as a ballot box — but the exact length of a sentence, down to which months are suspended or backdated, can decide whether a frontrunner is knocked out or set free. Details are destiny.

stories.moot: In modern democracies a courtroom can matter as much as a ballot box — a single ruling can remove a frontrunner and reroute a country's politics, which is why 'political risk' includes judicial calendars.

stories.title: France decides tomorrow whether Marine Le Pen can run for president
stories.moot: In modern democracies, a courtroom can matter as much as a ballot box — a single ruling can remove a frontrunner and reroute a country's politics, which is why 'political risk' includes judicial calendars.

stories.title: Europe's deadly heat: France records ~2,025 excess deaths as June records shatter
stories.moot: Climate change shows up first as heat deaths and higher electricity bills — the costs are already here and being paid, not hypothetical or far in the future.

china.name: China
china.primer: An AI 'model' is the underlying brain behind chatbots like ChatGPT or Claude — software trained on huge amounts of text to answer questions, write and reason. Its rough size is measured in 'parameters', the internal dials it tunes while learning; more parameters can mean more capability, but also far more computing power (and expensive chips) to build and run. Until now the most powerful models came from a few US firms — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google — and were kept closed. Moonshot AI is a Chinese startup (backed by e-commerce giant Alibaba and founded by researcher Yang Zhilin) whose 'Kimi' chatbot is popular in China. This launch echoes the 'DeepSeek moment' of early 2025, when another Chinese lab shocked markets by matching US models at a fraction of the cost.

stories.title: China's Moonshot AI unveils Kimi K3 — a giant, cheap open model that rivals America's best and triggers a 'DeepSeek moment' chip selloff
stories.moot: When a capable competitor shows up at a fraction of the price, it can reprice an entire industry overnight — because a lot of a market leader's value rests on the belief that its product is scarce and hard to copy. 'Good enough and much cheaper' is often more disruptive than 'the best', and in a gold rush the companies selling the shovels (here, chipmakers) can fall fastest when people suspect the digging just got cheaper.

stories.title: China's economy grows just 4.3% — its slowest pace since 2022 — as a slump in investment and cautious shoppers drag on the world's No. 2 economy
stories.moot: An economy that grows by building — endless roads, flats and factories — eventually runs out of useful things to build, and growth stalls until spending shifts to consumers. China's struggle shows how hard it is to switch from an investment-led model to one powered by ordinary households, and why a slowdown in one giant economy quietly reprices commodities and shares all over the world.

stories.title: The world just tilted toward Beijing: for the first time in about 20 years of polling, more countries view China favourably than the United States, Pew finds — China leads in 25 of 36 nations
stories.moot: Soft power is a national asset you can spend or squander, and it moves with behaviour: alienate allies and it erodes quietly, letting a rival gain ground without firing a shot — because in global affairs, being trusted is itself a form of leverage.

stories.title: China's DeepSeek raises money at a $74bn valuation — and its founder quietly becomes the world's richest AI-startup billionaire
stories.moot: Cutting off a rival's tools slows them down, but it also forces them to build their own — restrictions can accelerate the very self-reliance they were meant to prevent.

stories.title: China's economy grows just 4.3% — its slowest pace since 2022 and its first missed target since Covid, as weak demand at home offsets a booming export machine
stories.moot: Strong headline exports can hide a weak economy underneath. Growth has two engines — what a country sells abroad and what its own people and firms spend at home — and when the domestic engine stalls, foreign sales alone can't keep the whole machine at full speed. A missed official target is often the first public admission that the weakness is real.

stories.title: The chip-war line blurs: US confirms the first (so far tiny) shipments of Nvidia's H200 AI chips to China
stories.moot: Export controls are a dial, not a switch. A government can loosen or tighten the flow of sensitive technology — and even turn permission into a revenue stream — trading national-security caution against commercial and fiscal gain. Watch the volume and the terms, not just the yes-or-no headline.

stories.title: China's exports jump 27% in June as the AI boom drives chip demand - the other side of the AI trade
stories.moot: A boom has a supply side as well as a stock-price side. The clearest read on whether AI demand is real isn't a soaring share price - it's the physical flow of chips and hardware showing up in a big exporter's trade data.

stories.title: China expels Politburo member Ma Xingrui in a widening purge
stories.moot: In a system without open elections or a free press, big personnel moves — who gets promoted, who gets purged — are the main public signal of where power actually sits, so outsiders read the politics by watching them.

stories.title: Volkswagen warns of up to 50,000 more job cuts to survive
stories.moot: In a fast technology shift, incumbents are often crippled not by running out of money but by a cost structure built for the old world — high wages and legacy factories become an anchor when nimbler rivals redefine the product.

stories.title: China evacuates nearly two million as Typhoon Bavi hits Zhejiang
stories.moot: Warmer oceans hand storms more energy, so the same weather system can grow larger and wetter than it once would have. And the smartest way to survive a big storm is to move people before it arrives — the cost of relocating millions is far smaller than the cost of not.

stories.title: India's imports from China hit a record ~$80bn in the first half of 2026
stories.moot: Economic dependence and political rivalry can grow at the same time — supply chains often ignore borders and headlines, binding countries together even as their governments pull apart.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi makes landfall in China — nearly two million evacuated in a second storm within a week
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk — a storm's path over a manufacturing hub can matter as much to global supply chains as any boardroom decision, which is why markets now watch the forecast near places like Taiwan and China's coast.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi makes landfall in eastern China — up to 2 million evacuated in a second storm in a week
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk — a storm's path over a manufacturing hub can matter as much to global supply chains as any boardroom decision, which is why markets now watch the forecast near places like Taiwan and China's coast.

stories.title: China weighs curbing overseas access to its AI models — a new front in the tech cold war
stories.moot: Any input a country dominates can become a lever of power — first it was chips and oil, now it may be AI models. When one side controls something the other needs, access itself becomes a bargaining chip.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi batters East Asia — China evacuates hundreds of thousands after 15 die in Philippine landslides
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk — a storm's path over a manufacturing hub can matter as much to global supply chains as any factory decision, which is why markets increasingly watch the forecast.

stories.title: China fires a long-range missile into the South Pacific — a nuclear message aimed at Washington
stories.moot: Weapons tests are often messages, not just drills — a state chooses when and where to demonstrate capability to shape rivals' calculations, so the timing and audience tell you as much as the hardware.

stories.title: China cracks open the door to Nvidia's H200 chips — and Nvidia jumps against a red market
stories.moot: In a market led by one theme, news that widens that theme's addressable market can lift a single stock even against a falling tape — but policy-driven gains are only as durable as the policy, which can flip with a headline.

stories.title: India opens an anti-dumping probe into cheap Chinese electric tractors
stories.moot: Trade openness and industrial ambition constantly pull against each other — cheap imports help buyers but can hollow out a nascent domestic industry, so governments reach for anti-dumping duties to buy their own manufacturers time.

stories.title: China promotes new generals after a purge — as Western worry over its Russia ties grows
stories.moot: In autocracies, 'anti-corruption' campaigns double as loyalty tools — who gets purged and who gets promoted tells you where power is really consolidating.

united-kingdom.name: United Kingdom
united-kingdom.primer: Britain does not directly elect its prime minister. Voters elect Members of Parliament (MPs); whoever leads the party with a majority in the House of Commons becomes PM. Labour, the main centre-left party, won a big majority in 2024 under Keir Starmer, but his government struggled with weak economic growth, a high cost of living and falling popularity. After Labour was hammered in local elections in May 2026 and his own MPs began to rebel, Starmer agreed to step aside. Andy Burnham — the popular, plain-speaking mayor of Greater Manchester, nicknamed the 'King of the North' for championing England's regions — emerged as his successor, winning a by-election last month to get the seat in Parliament that a PM must hold.

stories.title: Britain gets a new prime minister without an election: Andy Burnham is declared Labour leader and will replace Keir Starmer in Downing Street on Monday
stories.moot: In a parliamentary democracy the prime minister is simply the leader of the ruling party, not a directly elected president — so a country can change its head of government in the middle of a term, without any national election, the moment the governing party picks a new chief. And rapid leadership churn (seven prime ministers in ten years) is usually a symptom, not a cause: it reflects deeper economic strain and public frustration that no single leader has been able to fix.

stories.title: Britain nationalises British Steel — the government seizes its last major steel mill from its Chinese owner to keep the country able to make its own steel
stories.moot: Some industries are treated as strategic, not just commercial: when a country decides it must be able to make its own steel, energy or chips, it will pay to keep that capability even if the business itself loses money. The market alone doesn't protect things a nation considers essential to its security — which is why governments sometimes step in and buy them.

stories.title: Argentina reaches the World Cup final by beating England 2-1 — then unfurls a 'Malvinas are Argentine' banner that could trigger a FIFA punishment
stories.moot: Global sporting stages are among the most powerful megaphones on earth, so the rules that try to keep politics off the pitch keep colliding with the fact that players carry their nations' grievances onto it — and enforcing 'no politics' is far harder than declaring it.

stories.title: India's biggest trade deal with the West goes live: the India-UK free-trade pact kicks in — cheaper Scotch and British cars coming in, near-zero tariffs for Indian exports going out
stories.moot: Free-trade deals work by trading away protection: each side gives up some of its tariffs to win access to the other's market. The winners are exporters and the consumers of the newly cheaper goods; the real test is always whether the jobs and sales gained outweigh the home industries newly exposed to competition.

stories.title: Britain is about to swap prime ministers again: Keir Starmer resigns and Andy Burnham is set to take over on Monday — the UK's 7th leader in just over a decade
stories.moot: In a parliamentary democracy a prime minister doesn't need to lose a national election to lose power — they govern only as long as their own party's MPs back them. When the party turns, the leader falls, and the country's top job can change hands without voters casting a single ballot.

stories.title: Ann Widdecombe, a well-known British politician, is killed in a 'targeted attack' — and counter-terror police take over the case
stories.moot: Political violence is measured not just by the one life lost but by the fear it spreads — when a public figure is attacked, the real target is open debate itself, which is why states treat such acts as terrorism rather than ordinary crime.

stories.title: Over 2,700 may have died in England and Wales's May–June heatwaves
stories.moot: The deadliest weather is often the least dramatic — heat kills quietly, mostly the old and frail indoors, so societies underprepare for it. As the climate warms, the number that matters is not the record temperature but the body count it leaves behind.

stories.title: India's Air India crash probe enters its final stage: investigators finish the cockpit-voice transcript and a 'psychological autopsy,' with the draft report due in October
stories.moot: Air-crash investigations are deliberately slow and layered — machines, procedures and people are all examined before any cause is named. Early leaks and speculation almost always outrun the evidence, which is exactly why final reports take a year or more and why an unproven theory should never be treated as the conclusion.

stories.title: India's biggest trade deal in years goes live: the India–UK free-trade pact (CETA) takes effect today, targeting ~$100bn in two-way trade by 2030
stories.moot: A trade deal's value isn't in the signing — it's in the switch-on. Tariffs are a hidden tax that quietly prices exporters out of foreign markets; removing them can hand an industry a lasting cost edge. And spreading trade across many partners is itself a form of insurance and bargaining power — the more doors a country can sell through, the less any single partner can dictate terms.

stories.title: India beat England by 6 wickets in 1st ODI as Gill and Axar star
stories.moot: In limited-overs cricket, players who can both bat and bowl are disproportionately valuable — one all-rounder having a big day, as Axar Patel did, can swing a match on both sides of the ball.

stories.title: India's trade win lands tomorrow: the 'most ambitious' India–UK free-trade deal comes into force July 15, cutting UK tariffs on 99.5% of trade value
stories.moot: Trade negotiation is a game of alternatives — the more partners a country can deal with, the less any single one can dictate terms. India banking a big UK deal while holding firm with the US shows how diversification itself becomes bargaining power, and how durable access is built deal by deal beneath the daily noise.

stories.title: Heat and fire on two continents — wildfire reaches Paris's outskirts, a 'dangerous' US heat dome builds, and 2,700 heat deaths counted in England and Wales
stories.moot: A warming climate turns ordinary 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can kill thousands indoors, burn suburbs, strain grids and threaten harvests at once, because societies and infrastructure were built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: India's women make history at Lord's — a first-ever Test win there, by 270 runs over England
stories.moot: Landmark moments on iconic stages are how emerging sports scale — a single high-profile win can pull in sponsors, viewers and young talent, compounding a team's rise well beyond the result itself.

stories.title: Deadly heat spans two continents — Spain's wildfire kills 12+ as a US heat dome bakes two-thirds of the country
stories.moot: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can kill in a wildfire, close landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids across whole continents at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Europe's heat emergency turns deadly — Spanish wildfire kills 12+, Paris shuts the Eiffel Tower early
stories.moot: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same heatwave can kill in a wildfire, close national landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: India–UK free-trade pact takes effect July 15 — 99% of Indian exports go duty-free
stories.moot: Free-trade deals reward the details — the tariff headline grabs attention, but it's the 'rules of origin' paperwork that decides who actually captures the benefit.

asia-pacific.name: Asia-Pacific
asia-pacific.primer: Crowded nightlife venues with few exits, flammable interiors and lax fire enforcement have caused repeated mass-casualty fires across Southeast Asia. In a packed bar, the danger is rarely the flames alone but the smoke, the crush, and — fatally — exits that are locked or blocked. A fire tore through a music bar in Bangkok's Chatuchak district over the weekend.

stories.title: Bangkok bar fire: toll stands at 30 in the city's deadliest fire in 17 years, with locked doors confirmed
stories.moot: In a crowded indoor space, survival is decided by design and enforcement — how many exits work, how flammable the room is, whether the rules were followed — long before any fire starts. The spark is the trigger; the building decisions are the cause.

stories.title: At least 28 killed in a Bangkok bar fire — patrons ran through flames to escape
stories.moot: In a crowded indoor space, safety is decided before the fire ever starts — by the number of exits, the flammability of the fit-out and whether rules were enforced. The death toll of a fire is often set by building decisions made years earlier, not by the spark itself.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi makes landfall in China — nearly two million evacuated in a second storm within a week
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk — a storm's path over a manufacturing hub can matter as much to global supply chains as any boardroom decision, which is why markets now watch the forecast near places like Taiwan and China's coast.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi makes landfall in eastern China — up to 2 million evacuated in a second storm in a week
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk — a storm's path over a manufacturing hub can matter as much to global supply chains as any boardroom decision, which is why markets now watch the forecast near places like Taiwan and China's coast.

stories.title: Modi upgrades India–New Zealand ties to a 'strategic partnership' — first Indian PM visit in 40 years
stories.moot: Diplomacy compounds like interest — a run of back-to-back deals (missiles, uranium, trade, partnerships) turns individual handshakes into a strategic web, and the best time to court neglected partners is when a rival's rise makes every friend more valuable.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi batters East Asia — China evacuates hundreds of thousands after 15 die in Philippine landslides
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk — a storm's path over a manufacturing hub can matter as much to global supply chains as any factory decision, which is why markets increasingly watch the forecast.

stories.title: Modi's Australia visit lands a prize: a uranium supply deal for India's nuclear push
stories.moot: The best time to reduce a dependency is when a crisis exposes it — a war in the oil lanes is precisely what pushes an importer to lock in alternative fuels and diversify supply, turning a scare into a structural shift.

stories.title: China fires a long-range missile into the South Pacific — a nuclear message aimed at Washington
stories.moot: Weapons tests are often messages, not just drills — a state chooses when and where to demonstrate capability to shape rivals' calculations, so the timing and audience tell you as much as the hardware.

stories.title: Deadly heat and storms: a Spanish wildfire kills 12 as Typhoon Bavi bears down on Japan
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk: extreme weather is no longer just a humanitarian story but a driver of insurance costs, farm output and even chip supply — which is why markets increasingly watch the forecast.

stories.title: Modi flies from Jakarta to Melbourne — an India–Australia summit on trade, defence and critical minerals
stories.moot: Middle powers grow influence by weaving many partnerships at once — arms with one country, minerals with another, trade with a third — so their security and supply chains never depend on a single friend or rival.

stories.title: Modi seals a BrahMos-missile pact in Jakarta and collects Indonesia's highest honour
stories.moot: Middle powers grow influence by weaving overlapping webs — arms, minerals, payments — so no single partner can hold them hostage. Modern diplomacy is measured in signed supply chains as much as in handshakes.

stories.title: Modi lands in Jakarta — BrahMos missiles, nickel and a UPI–QRIS payments link on the table
stories.moot: Middle powers grow their influence by building overlapping webs — arms, minerals, payments — so no single partner can hold them hostage. Diplomacy today is as much about supply chains and standards as about handshakes.

gaza.name: Gaza
gaza.primer: Hamas, the armed Islamist movement, has run Gaza's civil administration since 2007. A US-backed peace framework — mediated by Egypt, Qatar and Turkey and backed by a UN Security Council resolution — envisions Hamas stepping back from governance and eventually disarming, with a neutral committee of technocrats (unelected experts) running day-to-day life while Gaza is rebuilt.

stories.title: Overshadowed by the Iran war, Israel now controls nearly 70% of Gaza as hospitals go dark
stories.moot: A ceasefire is a label, not a guarantee — what matters is the reality underneath it: who controls the ground and whether basic systems like power and hospitals still function; watch conditions, not just declarations.

stories.title: Nine months into a ceasefire, Israel now controls nearly 70% of Gaza as hospitals go dark
stories.moot: A ceasefire is a label, not a guarantee — what matters is the reality underneath it: who controls the ground and whether basic systems like power and hospitals still function; watch conditions, not just declarations.

stories.title: Hamas dissolves its Gaza government, handing power to a technocratic committee
stories.moot: In peace deals, the sequence is everything: handing over administration is far easier than handing over weapons, so the governance step can advance while the security core stays frozen — progress and deadlock at the same time.

turkey.name: Turkey
turkey.primer: NATO is the 32-nation military alliance that binds the US, Canada and most of Europe under a promise of mutual defence. At last year's summit Trump extracted a pledge that members would raise defence spending toward 5% of GDP (the size of their economies) — a huge jump. This year's summit, in Turkey's capital Ankara, is where he tries to make them honour it. It convenes days after Russia's heaviest barrage of the war exposed that Ukraine's US-made Patriot air-defence systems — the only weapon that can reliably stop Russia's fastest 'ballistic' missiles — have almost run out of interceptor missiles.

stories.title: NATO summit wraps: €70bn for Ukraine, and Trump lets Kyiv build its own Patriot missiles
stories.moot: Owning the factory beats owning the stockpile — a licence to build your own weapons changes a country's leverage far more than any single shipment, but manufacturing capacity takes years to stand up, so the relief lags the announcement.

stories.title: NATO's Ankara summit pledges €70bn for Ukraine — but much of it is old money, and the US won't pay
stories.moot: Read the fine print on big numbers: a headline aid figure can be mostly old money relabelled, and who actually writes the cheque (here, Europe, not the US) tells you more about real commitment than the total does.

stories.title: NATO summit opens in Ankara — Trump demands the money, Ukraine begs for interceptors after its worst night
stories.moot: Alliances are tested not by their promises but by their invoices — a summit reveals how much collective defence a group will actually pay for, and on the battlefield the side that can still shoot down incoming missiles keeps its bargaining power.

taiwan.name: Taiwan
taiwan.primer: TSMC (Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company) is the world's most important chipmaker — a 'foundry' that manufactures the advanced processors designed by others, from Apple's iPhone chips to Nvidia's AI accelerators. Roughly nine in ten of the world's most cutting-edge chips are made in its factories, almost all of them in Taiwan. That concentration has become a geopolitical worry: Taiwan sits in China's shadow, and Washington has pushed TSMC to build plants on American soil to secure supply. TSMC had already committed about $165bn to US factories in Arizona; today's move stacks another $100bn on top.

stories.title: TSMC posts a record quarter and pledges another $100bn to build chips in America — lifting its US bet to $265bn as the AI boom rages
stories.moot: When the company that manufactures nearly all the world's most advanced chips posts record profits and raises its spending, it's a powerful real-world gauge that the AI boom is still building — the 'picks-and-shovels' supplier often reveals more about a gold rush than the miners do. And where critical factories get built is as much about geopolitics as economics.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi makes landfall in China — nearly two million evacuated in a second storm within a week
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk — a storm's path over a manufacturing hub can matter as much to global supply chains as any boardroom decision, which is why markets now watch the forecast near places like Taiwan and China's coast.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi makes landfall in eastern China — up to 2 million evacuated in a second storm in a week
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk — a storm's path over a manufacturing hub can matter as much to global supply chains as any boardroom decision, which is why markets now watch the forecast near places like Taiwan and China's coast.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi batters East Asia — China evacuates hundreds of thousands after 15 die in Philippine landslides
stories.moot: Climate risk is economic risk — a storm's path over a manufacturing hub can matter as much to global supply chains as any factory decision, which is why markets increasingly watch the forecast.

stories.title: Typhoon Bavi's aftermath begins on America's Pacific islands — Taiwan is next on the track
stories.moot: Warm oceans are storm fuel — as seas heat, the strongest storms become more common, and their economic bite depends on what lies on the track: a chip-making island in the path turns a weather story into a supply-chain one.

canada.name: Canada
canada.primer: Canada's boreal forest — the vast northern woodland that rings the top of the country — burns every summer, but the fires now start earlier and grow bigger as the climate warms and dries the land. In 2023, smoke from Canadian wildfires famously turned New York's skyline orange, showing how one country's forest fire becomes another country's public-health emergency. This week a fresh outbreak in northern Ontario, fanned by extreme heat and drought, has flared into one of the season's worst episodes.

stories.title: Trump threatens to make Canada pay for its wildfire smoke — through tariffs — as 'filthy' haze from record fires chokes US cities
stories.moot: Tariffs have become an all-purpose political weapon, reached for far beyond their original job of shaping trade — even aimed at things, like drifting smoke, that they cannot actually tax. The episode also shows how a warming climate turns natural disasters into diplomatic disputes: as cross-border harms like wildfire smoke grow, so do the fights over who is to blame and who should pay.

stories.title: Toronto's air becomes the world's worst as 100+ Ontario wildfires blanket Canada — and the smoke is now drifting over New York and Washington
stories.moot: A wildfire is a local event, but its smoke is a regional one — pollution travels hundreds of miles on the wind, so a fire in one country can send another country's cities indoors. As the climate warms, 'fire season' increasingly means 'smoke season' even for people who live nowhere near the flames.

stories.title: US launches a sweeping H-1B visa-fraud probe — Indian IT firms and workers in the spotlight
stories.moot: Cross-border industries depend on the rules of the country they serve — a labour-mobility programme like the H-1B is a policy lever, and tightening it can reshape an entire foreign industry's cost base and strategy without a single tariff being raised.

vietnam.name: Vietnam
vietnam.primer: Phu Quoc is Vietnam's largest island, a southern resort destination that has become increasingly popular with Indian tourists for holidays and group trips, with boat excursions a common activity. The victims in this incident were largely from Tamil Nadu and Kerala.

stories.title: 15 Indian tourists killed as a boat capsizes off Vietnam's Phu Quoc — Modi condoles, Hanoi orders a probe
stories.moot: Tourism safety is only as strong as its least-regulated operator: when a destination gets popular fast, visitor numbers can outrun the rules for boats, buses and guides — so one lapse can produce an outsized, cross-border tragedy.

stories.title: Vietnam orders a probe into the Phu Quoc boat capsize that killed 15 Indian tourists
stories.moot: As a country grows richer its citizens travel more, and 'traveller safety' abroad becomes a real test of the state — the speed and competence of an embassy's response is part of what a modern government is judged on.

stories.title: Boat tragedy off Vietnam's Phu Quoc kills 15 Indian tourists; Modi 'extremely saddened'
stories.moot: As a country grows richer its citizens travel more, and 'traveller safety' abroad becomes a real test of the state — the speed and competence of an embassy's response is part of what a modern government is judged on.

spain.name: Spain
spain.primer: Europe is enduring a prolonged summer of extreme heat that dries out vegetation and turns any spark into a fast-moving wildfire, with scientists linking the rising frequency to a warming climate. Fires once concentrated in the hotter south have reached the cooler, densely populated region around Paris — even the storied Fontainebleau forest.

stories.title: Summer of extremes: wildfire hits Paris's Fontainebleau forest, 10 still missing in Spain, and warm rivers cut French nuclear output
stories.moot: A warming climate turns ordinary 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can burn suburbs that never burned before, strain grids, cut power output and threaten harvests at once, because infrastructure was built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Deadly heat spans two continents — Spain's wildfire kills 12+ as a US heat dome bakes two-thirds of the country
stories.moot: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same pattern can kill in a wildfire, close landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids across whole continents at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.

stories.title: Europe's heat emergency turns deadly — Spanish wildfire kills 12+, Paris shuts the Eiffel Tower early
stories.moot: A warming climate turns 'hot weather' into a systemic shock — the same heatwave can kill in a wildfire, close national landmarks, buckle transport and stress power grids at once, because societies were built for a cooler normal.

thailand.name: Thailand
thailand.primer: Thailand, and Bangkok in particular, has a long record of deadly entertainment-venue fires — the worst being the 2009 Santika nightclub blaze that killed 66 people, roughly 17 years ago. After each disaster the government promises tighter enforcement of fire codes, but crowded bars with flammable decor, few marked exits and no sprinklers keep reappearing. The Rong Beer Na Ladprao was a popular live-music bar in the north of the capital that advertised space for as many as 600 customers.

stories.title: Bangkok's deadliest fire in 17 years: at least 32 killed at a packed live-music bar as victims are found trapped in windowless bathrooms
stories.moot: In a fire, people die from blocked escape and toxic smoke far more often than from the flames themselves — which is why marked, unobstructed exits and non-flammable materials save more lives than any amount of firefighting speed. The same fatal pattern (overcrowding + flammable interiors + too few exits) repeats in venue fires across the world, so it is preventable, not freak bad luck.

stories.title: Bangkok bar fire: toll stands at 30 in the city's deadliest fire in 17 years, with locked doors confirmed
stories.moot: In a crowded indoor space, survival is decided by design and enforcement — how many exits work, how flammable the room is, whether the rules were followed — long before any fire starts. The spark is the trigger; the building decisions are the cause.

stories.moot: In a crowded indoor space, safety is decided before the fire ever starts — by the number of exits, the flammability of the fit-out and whether rules were enforced. The death toll of a fire is often set by building decisions made years earlier, not by the spark itself.
